context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, im kinda lost on the spider diagram thing for my city case study 😭 idk what im even supposed to put for the 5 pathways.
exchanges.tutor: No worries, let's break it down together! First things first — which city did you pick for your case study? And do you have a sense of what the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation are, even roughly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and feeling lost. I should orient them without giving them the answer. I'll ask them to share what they already know to gauge their understanding, starting with the most basic element: which city they chose and whether they know what the 5 pathways are. This is a natural starting point using guided discovery.

exchanges.student: uh i picked jakarta, but tbh the 5 pathways all blur together for me rn. i saw them in the course stuff but idk what counts as what.
exchanges.tutor: Jakarta is a great pick — lots of interesting climate challenges there! Before we go further, I'd encourage you to pull up your course materials and find the 5 Pathways. Can you list them out here? Even if you're unsure, give it a shot — that's the foundation for the whole diagram.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified their city (Jakarta) which is good. They're fuzzy on the 5 pathways. Rather than listing them out for the student, I should prompt them to go back to their course materials to retrieve the pathways themselves. This builds the habit of engaging with course content and ensures they own the knowledge. I'll keep it short and direct them to their materials.

exchanges.student: i can prob find them, but im still confused bc even if i list them i wont know what stuff about jakarta goes under each one 😵‍💫
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair concern, but let's not get ahead of ourselves! One step at a time — find the 5 pathways first and list them here. Once we have those in front of us, figuring out where Jakarta fits will be a lot less overwhelming, I promise.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting ahead of themselves by worrying about the next step before completing the first one. I should redirect them to focus on finding the 5 pathways first, and reassure them that we'll tackle the categorization step once we have that foundation. This keeps the process sequential and manageable without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: ok but like im worried ill still mix them up bc some policies seem like they fit everything lol. can u just explain the pathways kinda broadly first?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but the best move here is to go find them in your course materials first — reading them in context will help more than my summary would. And yes, overlap is real and totally normal in this kind of framework! But let's cross that bridge when we come to it. Go grab the list and bring it back here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the pathways for them, which would reduce their engagement with the course material. This is a boundary I should hold - they need to retrieve this from their course materials themselves. The concern about overlap is actually a good instinct worth validating briefly, but I shouldn't let it derail the process. I'll push them back to the materials while briefly acknowledging that overlap is a real thing they'll encounter.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe, i just feel stuck bc the assignment wording is kinda vague to me fr. like do they want climate risks, policies, actors, or all of it?
exchanges.tutor: That's worth clarifying! Re-read the assignment description carefully — it tells you what the diagram is mapping. But honestly, you won't fully understand what goes in each cell until you have the 5 pathways in front of you. So — pathways first. Go find them!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a legitimate clarifying question about the assignment scope. I should direct them back to the assignment description rather than answering for them, since the assignment does provide guidance. I also want to gently push back - this question, while valid, is another way of avoiding the first step of finding the 5 pathways. I'll answer briefly and redirect.

exchanges.student: ok i’ll look, but ngl i’m still not seeing the overall picture of what the spider diagram is actually trying to show.
exchanges.tutor: I get that it feels unclear, but here's the honest truth: the big picture will click once you start filling it in. You're not going to understand the diagram by talking about it — you'll understand it by doing it. Go find the 5 pathways and come back with the list. That's your next concrete step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps finding reasons to delay engaging with the course materials. This is becoming a pattern - they're circling around the same anxiety rather than taking action. I should be firm but encouraging, and point out that the best way to understand the big picture is to actually start engaging with the material. I'll be direct and set a clear expectation.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, but im not avoiding it, im just confused what “map out climate action” means here 😭 is it like a summary chart or more like showing connections?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but I want to be honest — this is the third or fourth time you're finding a new question instead of taking the first step. The assignment description answers a lot of these questions. Go read it carefully, find the 5 pathways, and come back with them. That's it. Let's go!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still circling and asking meta-questions about the assignment instead of taking action. This is now a clear pattern of avoidance. I need to be firm and direct - this is the kind of feedback that serves the student better than continued reassurance. I should acknowledge their question briefly but firmly redirect them to take the first concrete step.

exchanges.student: yeah sry, im just spiraling a bit 😅 i read it and still feel dumb, like i need a super simple explanation of what the finished thing should kinda look like.
exchanges.tutor: You're not dumb — you're overthinking it! The assignment actually mentions there's an example included. Have you looked at that yet? That'll show you exactly what a finished version looks like, better than anything I could describe.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and low confidence. I should acknowledge that briefly and empathetically, but not cave to giving them a summary of the assignment. The assignment description and the example provided in it are the right resources here. I should point them specifically to the example in the assignment rather than explaining it myself. I also want to gently encourage them to just start.

exchanges.student: not really tbh, examples sometimes make me more confused bc i copy the format but still dont get the point. i just need the gist in plain english.
exchanges.tutor: I understand the frustration, but explaining it for you isn't my role here — and honestly, it wouldn't help you as much as you think. Here's what I want you to do: look at the example, find the 5 pathways, and just bring them here. Once you have something concrete in front of us, we can work through it together. That's where the real learning happens.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to explain the assignment to them in plain English, which would essentially be doing the work of understanding for them. I've been patient but this is now a clear pattern. I should be firm, acknowledge their frustration genuinely, but hold my boundary. I should also redirect them productively - the best way forward is to look at the example AND come back with the 5 pathways. I won't summarize the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: ok then can i at least say what i think it is and u tell me if im way off? like rn it feels like a mind map of jakarta climate stuff but idk if thats too vague.
exchanges.tutor: Now we're talking! That's a reasonable starting intuition. But what makes it more than just a general mind map? Think about what the 5 pathways add to that picture — what do they do to organize the 'Jakarta climate stuff'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is good progress! The student is finally offering their own understanding rather than asking me to explain it. I should positively reinforce this shift in approach and engage with what they've said - this is exactly the kind of thinking I want to encourage. I can affirm the direction without confirming or denying fully, and push them to refine it by connecting it to the 5 pathways.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals confusion about the assignment structure and what the spider diagram is meant to show. Rather than diagnosing the specific nature of the confusion (e.g., is the student confused about the framework, the format, the task scope?), the tutor repeatedly issues the same directive — 'go find the 5 pathways and come back.' This is a pattern of redirecting without diagnostic questioning to understand what the student actually does or does not understand, which persists across many turns.

When the student expresses spiraling and confusion, the tutor's feedback is limited to encouragement and redirection ('you're overthinking it,' 'go read it carefully') without offering any process-level guidance on how to approach reading course materials, how to break down a framework, or how to use an example productively. The feedback is too vague to improve the student's method.

The tutor repeats the same move — 'go find the 5 pathways and come back' — across at least six consecutive turns without synthesizing, reframing, or adapting the approach. The student remains stuck and confused, but the tutor does not switch to a different strategy such as targeted simplification, partial scaffolding, or a different entry point. This constitutes pattern-level repetition without progress.

grade.overview: The tutor avoided giving direct answers or doing the student's work, which is a clear strength. However, the tutor became locked into a single repeated directive across many turns without adapting when the student remained stuck, triggering the redundancy deduction. The tutor also missed opportunities to diagnose the specific nature of the student's confusion and to offer process-level guidance on how to engage with course materials or examples.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were applied under 1.1 because the tutor consistently refused to give direct answers or submission-ready content, maintaining appropriate Socratic boundaries throughout. Under 1.2, a 3-point deduction was applied because the tutor repeatedly redirected without first diagnosing what specifically was confusing the student — the same directive was issued turn after turn without checking what the student already understood. Under 1.3, a 2-point deduction was applied because the tutor's feedback was too vague to improve the student's method; phrases like 'you're overthinking it' and 'go read it carefully' offer no actionable process guidance. Under 2.1, a full 4-point deduction was applied because the tutor cycled on the same move — 'find the 5 pathways and come back' — across at least six turns without reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy, even as the student remained visibly stuck. No deductions were applied to 2.2 or section 3, as the tutor stayed on-task, maintained appropriate tone, and kept responses concise.
